wikibase_candidates.label: Howard Gutstadt
wikibase_candidates.description: artist associated with E.A.T.’s Technical Services Program
wikibase_candidates.reasoning: Both records refer to Howard Gutstadt. The name is identical, and the card date (1969) aligns perfectly with the activity period of the E.A.T. Technical Services Program described in the Wikibase entity.

results.possibleIdentity: Howard Gutstadt is an American video artist, media producer, and educator who was a pioneering figure in the early video art and media activism movements of the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known as a co-founder of several influential New York-based video collectives, including Commediation (1968), People's Video Theater (PVT, 1970), and Survival Arts Media (SAM, 1972). His work often blended community activism with technical experimentation, utilizing early portable video equipment (Portapaks) to document counterculture movements, such as the first women's liberation and gay pride marches. Notably, Gutstadt was involved in filming the 'Camp Jened' tapes in 1971, which documented a summer camp for teenagers with disabilities and later served as the archival core for the 2020 documentary 'Crip Camp.' Gutstadt was also a key figure in the development of public access television in New York City, organizing the Public Access Celebration to coordinate video groups for the rollout of cable access. In the mid-1970s, he relocated to Jamestown, New York, where he continued his work with Survival Arts Media, focusing on immersive multimedia performances and electronic video synthesis using tools like the Rutt-Etra and EAB synthesizers. He later moved to California in 1978, establishing Digital Image Productions in Berkeley to develop digital video and animation software for artists, collaborating with companies such as Sony, Autodesk, and Diaquest. His work has been exhibited at venues including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Kitchen, and SIGGRAPH. The People's Video Theater / Survival Arts Media archive, which Gutstadt helped preserve, is now held at the NYU Special Collections Library.

webSearchQueries: "Howard Gutstadt" E.A.T. Experiments in Art and Technology

results.possibleIdentity: Daniel Weinberger (also known as Daniel von Weinberger) is a multidisciplinary Belgian artist born in Antwerp in 1950. He was actively developing his career in the late 1960s and early 1970s, having studied at the Royal Academy of Fine Arts Antwerp from 1965 to 1972. His work is noted for its diversity, spanning jewelry design, video art, installations, painting, and stage costumes. During the 1970s, he was part of the early wave of video artists in Europe; for instance, the Provinciaal Museum voor Moderne Kunst in Ypres began acquiring his video work during this period. His practice often blurs the lines between fine art and applied arts like jewelry, and he is recognized as a significant figure in the Antwerp art scene.

Janet Weinberger was a performer and collaborator in the New York avant-garde circle that pre-figured and overlapped with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. She is most notably recorded as a performer in Allan Kaprow's seminal 1959 Happening, "18 Happenings in 6 Parts," staged at the Reuben Gallery. This event was a foundational moment for the E.A.T. community, featuring future E.A.T. founders such as Robert Rauschenberg and Robert Whitman. In the performance, Weinberger was responsible for musical elements and movement, contributing to the experimental and collaborative nature of the group's early work into the 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Howard Brentlinger was an artist and librarian active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is identified as a member of the Department of Art faculty at Cornell University, where he participated in faculty exhibitions featuring painting, sculpture, print-making, and photography. In 1968, he served as the Director of the Tompkins County Public Library in Ithaca, New York. His association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is consistent with his presence in the Cornell academic community during a period when the organization promoted collaborations between artists and technical professionals. Brentlinger was also recognized for his contributions to the community through the Howard Brentlinger Memorial Book Fund established at the Tompkins County Public Library Foundation.

H. Brentlinger (referenced as Albert H. Brentlinger Jr. in some technical records) was an amateur radio enthusiast and technical specialist active in the 1960s and 1970s. Based in Colorado, he was a member of the Colorado Amateur Radio Weather Network and received public service awards for providing weather reports to the National Weather Service. Given the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization's focus on connecting artists with engineers and technical hobbyists—particularly those with expertise in communications technology—he represents a possible technical collaborator associated with the organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s.
